items.question_text: How do you establish the sequence of arriving and departing aircraft?

choices.text: Require aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.
Issue landing clearances in the order aircraft call the tower.
Use only ground delays and avoid airborne adjustments.
Let each pilot decide whether to extend, go around, or circle.

items.explanation: Sequencing is established by requiring aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as needed to achieve proper spacing.

items.instruction: Which maneuver is included in a cleared-for-the-option clearance?
items.question_text: Which maneuver is included?
items.explanation: The option clearance permits touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full-stop landing.

items.question_text: After ATC approves the option, the pilot should inform ATC as soon as possible of any delay on the runway during a stop-and-go or full-stop landing.
items.explanation: The note says the pilot should inform ATC as soon as possible of any runway delay during a stop-and-go or full-stop landing under an option clearance.

items.question_text: If proper helicopter spacing is needed, speed adjustments may be more practical than course changes.
items.explanation: The note says speed adjustments may be more practical than course changes for proper helicopter spacing.

items.situation: A following aircraft should be sequenced behind a Cessna on short final. Build the sequencing instruction.
items.target_phrase: NUMBER TWO FOLLOW THE CESSNA ON SHORT FINAL

items.situation: An instructor requests the option at a towered airport. Later in the pattern, a helicopter also needs spacing behind other traffic, and another controller says course changes are always better than speed changes for helicopters. The instructor may need extra runway time for a stop-and-go.
items.para_context: Sequencing is achieved by requiring flight or ground adjustments. The option clearance is available at locations with an operational control tower, subject to ATC approval, and pilots should advise ATC of runway delay during stop-and-go or full-stop landing. For helicopter spacing, speed adjustments may be more practical than course changes.

choices.text: Approve or deny the option based on ATC needs, expect the pilot to advise of any stop-and-go runway delay, and use whatever sequencing adjustment works best, including speed adjustment for the helicopter.
Approve the option automatically, prohibit any helicopter speed adjustment, and ignore possible stop-and-go delay because option traffic controls the runway.
Deny the option because towered airports may not use it, and use only 360s for helicopter spacing.
Approve the option only if the pilot agrees not to tell ATC about any runway delay, and use no sequence adjustments for the helicopter.

items.explanation: Option approval remains subject to ATC, runway delays should be reported as soon as possible, and helicopter spacing may be handled with speed adjustments when that is more practical.

items.question_text: For separation purposes, when does a touch-and-go aircraft stop being treated as an arriving aircraft?

choices.text: When it receives the touch-and-go clearance.
When it rotates for takeoff again.

items.explanation: A touch-and-go aircraft is considered arriving until touchdown and thereafter is considered departing.

items.instruction: Which event changes a low-approach aircraft from arriving to departing status?
items.question_text: Which event changes the status?

choices.text: Reaching the runway numbers on short final.
Climbing through 300 feet AGL after the low approach.

items.explanation: For a low approach, the aircraft remains arriving until it crosses the landing threshold and is thereafter treated as departing.

items.instruction: Is this stop-and-go status rule correct?
items.question_text: If an aircraft has been cleared for a stop-and-go and has not yet made a complete stop, you must still treat it as an arriving aircraft.
items.explanation: A stop-and-go aircraft remains an arriving aircraft until it makes a complete stop.

items.situation: One aircraft is cleared for touch-and-go, another is cleared for stop-and-go, and a third will make a low approach. Another controller wants to treat all three as departures as soon as the clearances are issued so the next departures can be moved more aggressively.
items.para_context: A touch-and-go remains an arrival until touchdown, a stop-and-go until complete stop, and a low approach until crossing the landing threshold. After that point each is treated as departing.

choices.text: Keep each aircraft in arriving status until its paragraph-specific transition point, then treat it as departing.
Treat all three as departures as soon as the clearances are issued because the next action after the maneuver is departure.
Treat all three as arrivals until they are abeam the departure end of the runway.
Treat only the low approach as an arrival and the others as departures immediately.

items.explanation: Each maneuver has its own arrival-to-departure transition point, and the aircraft is not treated as departing before that point.

items.question_text: What weather condition is required for simultaneous same-direction operations unless visual separation is applied?

choices.text: Any weather condition as long as the towers can see both aircraft.

items.explanation: Simultaneous same-direction operations require VFR conditions unless visual separation is applied.

items.question_text: Two-way radio communication must be maintained with the aircraft involved and pertinent traffic information must be issued.
items.explanation: Two-way radio communication and pertinent traffic information are both required conditions for SSDO.

items.question_text: If either aircraft is a Heavy, what minimum runway centerline spacing is required for simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-1 requires 700 feet between runway centerlines if either aircraft is a Heavy.

items.instruction: Which additional provision still applies during simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.question_text: Which provision still applies?

choices.text: Automatic visual separation without traffic information.

items.explanation: The note specifically says applicable wake turbulence provisions still apply during SSDO.

items.instruction: Is this Category III spacing rule correct?
items.question_text: If either aircraft is a Category III aircraft, you must have at least 500 feet between runway centerlines or 400 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip.
items.explanation: Table 3-8-1 requires 500 feet between centerlines or 400 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip if either aircraft is Category III.

items.situation: You are considering simultaneous same-direction operations on parallel runways in VFR. One aircraft is Category II and the other is Heavy. The runways are 650 feet apart centerline to centerline, and another controller argues that the 500-foot Category III standard is close enough because both aircraft are talking to the tower and the traffic is in sight.
items.para_context: SSDO requires VFR unless visual separation is applied, two-way radio communication, pertinent traffic information, and table minima based on aircraft category. If either aircraft is Heavy, the table requires 700 feet between runway centerlines.

choices.text: Do not authorize the operation on those runways because a Heavy requires 700 feet between runway centerlines.
Authorize it because 650 feet exceeds the Category III standard and the aircraft can see each other.
Authorize it because two-way radio communication replaces the runway-spacing requirement.
Authorize it only if wake turbulence is waived by both pilots.

items.explanation: The Heavy category drives the minima, and the table requires 700 feet between runway centerlines in that case.

items.question_text: What weather condition applies to simultaneous opposite-direction operations on parallel runways or landing strips?

choices.text: They must be conducted in VFR conditions.
They are allowed in any weather if traffic is reported in sight.
They require IFR conditions with radar separation.
They are limited to daylight only, regardless of runway spacing.

items.explanation: Simultaneous opposite-direction operations must be conducted in VFR conditions.

items.question_text: Two-way radio communication must be maintained with the aircraft involved and pertinent traffic information must be issued.
items.explanation: Those are stated conditions for simultaneous opposite-direction operations.

items.question_text: Between sunset and sunrise, what minimum runway centerline spacing is required for simultaneous opposite-direction operations?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-2 requires 2,800 feet between runway centerlines between sunset and sunrise.

items.situation: A Cessna is arriving opposite direction on a parallel runway and you need to issue the traffic advisory. Build the transmission.
items.target_phrase: TRAFFIC CESSNA ARRIVING OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY

items.instruction: One word in this traffic advisory is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: TRAFFIC CESSNA ARRIVING OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY.
items.display_text: TRAFFIC CESSNA ARRIVING SAME DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY
items.explanation: The approved phraseology says OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY/LANDING STRIP.

items.situation: You are considering simultaneous opposite-direction operations between sunset and sunrise on a runway and a parallel landing strip. The runway centerlines are 2,900 feet apart, but the edges of the adjacent strip and runway do not meet any nighttime authorization standard. Another controller argues the centerline spacing alone is enough because both aircraft are in radio contact.
items.para_context: Opposite-direction operations require VFR, two-way radio communication, pertinent traffic information, and the table minima. Between sunset and sunrise, Table 3-8-2 requires 2,800 feet between runway centerlines and says strip or runway-and-strip operations are not authorized.

choices.text: Do not authorize the runway-and-strip operation at night because strip or runway-and-strip opposite-direction operations are not authorized between sunset and sunrise.
Authorize it because the 2,900-foot centerline spacing satisfies the only nighttime requirement.
Authorize it if each pilot reports the other in sight, because that removes the strip restriction automatically.
Authorize it only if no traffic advisory is issued, because opposite-direction phraseology is optional at night.

items.explanation: At night the runway-centerline standard is 2,800 feet, but strip or runway-and-strip opposite-direction operations are not authorized at all.

items.question_text: How is the sequence of arriving and departing aircraft established?
items.explanation: The paragraph says sequence is established by requiring aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.

choices.text: By requiring aircraft to adjust flight or ground operation as necessary to achieve proper spacing.
By clearing each aircraft in first-call order.
By using only ground movement delays.
By limiting all sequencing changes to go-arounds.

items.question_text: What does a cleared-for-the-option clearance permit?
items.explanation: The option clearance permits a touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full-stop landing.

choices.text: A touch-and-go, low approach, missed approach, stop-and-go, or full-stop landing.
Any runway or pattern maneuver the pilot prefers.
Only a touch-and-go or full-stop landing.
Only a missed approach after touchdown.

items.question_text: A cleared-for-the-option clearance is used only at locations with an operational control tower and is subject to ATC approval.
items.explanation: That is the note's stated limit on the option procedure.

items.question_text: If a pilot approved for the option expects a runway delay during a stop-and-go or full-stop landing, what should happen?
items.explanation: The pilot should inform ATC as soon as possible of the delay.

choices.text: The pilot should inform ATC as soon as possible.
The pilot should wait until clear of the runway to mention it.
The pilot does not need to advise ATC because option approval reserves the runway.
The pilot must cancel the option and request a new clearance immediately.

items.question_text: What may be more practical than course changes for proper helicopter spacing?
items.explanation: The note says speed adjustments may be more practical than course changes.

items.question_text: When hold instructions are issued in lieu of a takeoff clearance, what read-back rule applies?
items.explanation: The note says read back of hold short instructions applies when hold instructions are issued in lieu of a takeoff clearance.

choices.text: Read back of hold short instructions applies.
No read back is needed because the aircraft remains in position.
Only the traffic information needs to be read back.
The aircraft must be reclassified as arriving.

items.question_text: For separation purposes, when does a touch-and-go aircraft stop being treated as an arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: It remains an arriving aircraft until it touches down.

choices.text: At the moment the clearance is issued.
When it crosses the landing threshold.
When it begins the takeoff roll after touch-and-go.

items.question_text: A stop-and-go aircraft remains an arriving aircraft until it makes a complete stop.
items.explanation: For stop-and-go, the arrival status continues until a complete stop is made.

items.question_text: When does a low-approach aircraft stop being treated as an arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: A low-approach aircraft is arriving until it crosses the landing threshold.

choices.text: When it crosses the landing threshold.
When it is cleared low approach.
When it passes the runway midpoint.
When it reports the runway in sight.

items.question_text: After the applicable transition point in 3-8-2, how is the aircraft treated?
items.explanation: After touchdown, complete stop, or threshold crossing as applicable, the aircraft is treated as departing.

choices.text: As an arriving aircraft until clear of the pattern.
As a vehicle on the runway for separation purposes.

items.question_text: Which event changes a stop-and-go aircraft from arriving to departing status?
items.explanation: The transition happens when the aircraft makes a complete stop.

items.question_text: What weather condition is required for simultaneous same-direction operations unless visual separation is applied?
items.explanation: The operations must be conducted in VFR conditions unless visual separation is applied.

choices.text: IFR conditions with tower visibility of both aircraft.
Any weather if the aircraft are the same category.

items.question_text: Two-way radio communication and pertinent traffic information are required for simultaneous same-direction operations.
items.explanation: Both are listed conditions for SSDO.

items.question_text: What minimum runway centerline spacing applies to Category I or Category II aircraft in simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-1 sets 300 feet between runway centerlines for Category I or Category II aircraft.

items.question_text: If either aircraft is Category III, what minimum spacing applies between adjacent strips or runway and strip?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-1 sets 400 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip if either aircraft is Category III.

items.question_text: If either aircraft is Heavy, what minimum spacing applies between edges of adjacent strips or runway and strip?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-1 sets 600 feet between adjacent strips or runway and strip if either aircraft is Heavy.

items.question_text: What additional provisions still apply during simultaneous same-direction operations?
items.explanation: The note says applicable wake turbulence provisions still apply during SSDO.

items.question_text: What weather condition applies to simultaneous opposite-direction operations on parallel runways or landing strips?
items.explanation: The paragraph says they must be conducted in VFR conditions.

items.question_text: Which advisory identifies opposite-direction traffic on the parallel runway or landing strip?
items.explanation: The phraseology includes OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY/LANDING STRIP.

choices.text: TRAFFIC CESSNA ARRIVING OPPOSITE DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY.
TRAFFIC CESSNA ARRIVING SAME DIRECTION ON PARALLEL RUNWAY.
TRAFFIC CESSNA LANDING BEHIND YOU ON PARALLEL RUNWAY.
TRAFFIC CESSNA DEPARTING LEFT BASE ON PARALLEL RUNWAY.

items.question_text: Between sunrise and sunset, what minimum runway centerline spacing applies to simultaneous opposite-direction operations?
items.explanation: Table 3-8-2 sets 1,400 feet between runway centerlines between sunrise and sunset.

items.question_text: Between sunset and sunrise, what does Table 3-8-2 say about opposite-direction operations between edges of adjacent strips or runway and strip?
items.explanation: At night, strip or runway-and-strip opposite-direction operations are not authorized.

choices.text: Authorized only for Category I aircraft.

items.question_text: Two-way radio communication and pertinent traffic information are required for simultaneous opposite-direction operations.
items.explanation: Those are stated conditions for opposite-direction operations.

items.question_text: What operations does 3-8-4 cover when the conditions are met?
items.explanation: The paragraph covers opposite-direction operations on parallel runways, on parallel landing strips, or on a runway and a parallel landing strip.

choices.text: Parallel runways, parallel landing strips, or a runway and a parallel landing strip.
Only parallel runways with the same magnetic heading.
Only opposite-direction operations on intersecting runways.
Only operations involving helicopters and fixed-wing traffic.
